stem: Consider \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \left(\dfrac{[[a]]n + ([[b]])}{[[c]]n + ([[d]])}\right)^{n} \). Apply the root test: compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} \).
solution: Since \( a_n \) is an \( n \)th power, \( \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} = \left|\dfrac{[[a]]n + ([[b]])}{[[c]]n + ([[d]])}\right| \to \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[c]]} \). Thus \( L = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the leading coefficients of the base.
The base tends to a nonzero limit, so \( L \) is that ratio, not \( 0 \).
This is off by one from the correct root-test limit.
This is off by one from the correct root-test limit.
This is off by two from the correct root-test limit.
This is off by two from the correct root-test limit.

stem: Consider \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \left(\dfrac{[[a]]n + ([[b]])}{n^{2} + [[p]]n + ([[q]])}\right)^{n} \). Apply the root test: compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} \).
solution: Since \( a_n \) is an \( n \)th power, \( \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} = \left|\dfrac{[[a]]n + ([[b]])}{n^{2} + [[p]]n + ([[q]])}\right| \). The denominator grows like \( n^{2} \) while the numerator grows only like \( n \), so the base tends to \( 0 \). Thus \( L = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly treats numerator and denominator as the same degree and reports the numerator's leading coefficient.
This adds unrelated coefficients from the numerator and denominator instead of comparing growth rates.
This subtracts unrelated coefficients from the numerator and denominator instead of comparing growth rates.
This is off by one from the correct root-test limit.
This is off by one from the correct root-test limit.
This is off by two from the correct root-test limit.
This is off by two from the correct root-test limit.

stem: Consider \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \left(\dfrac{n + [[b]]}{n}\right)^{[[c]]n^{2}} \). Apply the root test: compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} \).
solution: Since \( a_n \) is an \( n \)th power, \( \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} = \left(\dfrac{n+[[b]]}{n}\right)^{[[c]]n} = \left(1+\dfrac{[[b]]}{n}\right)^{[[c]]n} \to e^{[[b]]\cdot[[c]]} \) as \( n\to\infty \). Thus \( L = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the base's limit \( n/n=1 \) for the whole exponential limit, ignoring the growing exponent.
This drops the exponent's coefficient \( c \).
This drops the base's shift \( b \).
This adds \( b \) and \( c \) instead of multiplying them in the exponent of \( e \).
This forgets to exponentiate with \( e \) at all.
This is off by one from the correct root-test limit.
This is off by one from the correct root-test limit.

stem: Consider \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \) with \( a_n = \dfrac{[[c]]^{n}\,n!}{n^{n}} \). Apply the root test: compute \( L = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} \).
solution: Since \( a_n = \left(\dfrac{[[c]]\,(n!)^{1/n}}{n}\right)^{n} \), \( \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} = \dfrac{[[c]]\,(n!)^{1/n}}{n} \). By Stirling's approximation \( (n!)^{1/n} \sim n/e \), so this tends to \( \dfrac{[[c]]}{e} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of \( 1/e \) coming from \( (n!)^{1/n} \sim n/e \).
This wrongly assumes \( n! \) is negligible compared to \( n^{n} \), giving \( 0 \) instead of the correct finite ratio.
This uses \( 1/2 \) instead of \( 1/e \) as the asymptotic factor.
This multiplies by \( e \) instead of dividing by it.
This is off by one from the correct root-test limit.
This is off by one from the correct root-test limit.
